verdict: 2026-07-20 08:45:20 PDT — The clamp is idiomatic, dependency-free, and consistent with the repo, but the sketch does not yet satisfy its stated invariant for every input. I would keep Math.max and centralize it across both return paths after clarifying AC3’s contradictory scope.

findings.title: The pass-through branch bypasses the required clamp
findings.claim: The proposed Math.max applies only when stepSeconds >= 1. For stepSeconds below 1 or invalid, quantizeForDisplay still returns tc directly, so a negative totalCost remains negative. That violates AC1’s explicit “for any inputs” requirement and leaves the invariant split across return paths.
findings.alternative: Compute the candidate displayed total for either branch, then apply Math.max(0, candidateTotal) once at the return boundary; alternatively, clamp tc explicitly in the early return as well.
findings.win: Centralizes the non-negative output invariant and eliminates the bypass case with no dependency or new abstraction.

findings.title: AC1 and AC3 conflict for valid non-negative arguments
findings.claim: AC1 requires clamping every negative result, while AC3 says bucket behavior must remain identical for non-negative inputs. Those cannot both hold for inputs such as totalCost=1, elapsedSeconds=19, costPerSecond=2, stepSeconds=10: today’s result is -17, while the required clamp yields 0, despite every input being non-negative.
findings.alternative: Define AC3 as unchanged only when the pre-clamp quantized result is already non-negative, or explicitly restrict it to coherent accrual states where totalCost is at least the within-bucket walk-back amount. Make the regression test reflect that boundary.
findings.win: Removes an impossible behavioral contract and gives the implementation and regression test one precise invariant.
